does: This paper introduces the first formal study of machine unlearning in offline stochastic multi-armed bandits, formalizing privacy constraints for this sequential decision-making setting and proposing algorithmic solutions. The authors develop both single-source and multi-source unlearning approaches under fixed-sample and distribution data-generation models. Their core contribution consists of adaptive algorithms that strategically switch between two canonical base methods—the Gaussian mechanism and rollback—depending on the data regime and privacy budget. They introduce a mixing procedure that provides theoretical justification for these baseline approaches and establish performance guarantees characterizing the trade-off between privacy and decision quality in the post-unlearning policy.
connects: This work extends machine unlearning beyond its prior focus on supervised and unsupervised settings to the sequential decision-making domain. It builds on foundational unlearning frameworks that aim to remove individual data points' influence without full model retraining, addressing privacy and data-deletion requests. The paper connects to offline contextual bandit and multi-armed bandit literature by adapting these sequential decision problems to the unlearning setting. It leverages established privacy-utility techniques including differential privacy mechanisms and rollback strategies, while applying them to the novel challenge of maintaining policy quality after selective data removal in bandit environments.
enables: This work opens the door to privacy-preserving sequential decision systems, enabling future research on unlearning in more complex decision-making settings like contextual bandits, reinforcement learning, and online learning algorithms. The formalization and algorithmic techniques could support practical applications where systems must process user data-deletion requests while maintaining recommendation quality or control performance. The mixing procedure and adaptive algorithm design provide foundational insights that may generalize to other sequential or online learning problems requiring selective unlearning. This work sets up investigation of unlearning under non-stationary environments, longer horizons, and exploration-exploitation trade-offs fundamental to real-world bandit applications.
